name: Past Tense — Preterite
summary: Talk about completed past actions — what happened yesterday, last week, or at a specific time. The essential past tense for storytelling.
explanation:
The preterite (pretérito indefinido) is used for actions that started and finished at a specific time in the past. Think of it as the 'snapshot' tense — it captures completed events: 'Ayer comí paella' (Yesterday I ate paella), 'Viajé a España en 2023' (I travelled to Spain in 2023).

-AR verbs take the endings: -é, -aste, -ó, -amos, -asteis, -aron. -ER and -IR verbs share: -í, -iste, -ió, -imos, -isteis, -ieron. Notice that -AR and -ER/-IR nosotros forms look identical to the present tense — context tells you which tense is meant.

Several common verbs are irregular in the preterite: ir/ser (fui, fuiste, fue, fuimos, fuisteis, fueron — yes, ir and ser have the same preterite forms!), hacer (hice, hiciste, hizo...), estar (estuve...), tener (tuve...), poder (pude...), poner (puse...). These must be memorised individually.

examples.en: What did you eat last night?
We went to the cinema on Saturday

examples.es: Ella vivió en París tres años
examples.en: She lived in Paris for three years
I did the shopping this morning

examples.es: No pude ir a la fiesta
examples.en: I couldn't go to the party

commonMistakes: Forgetting accent marks: 'hable' (subjunctive) vs 'hablé' (preterite) — the accent matters
Using present tense for past events: 'Ayer como paella' should be 'Ayer comí paella'
Not knowing ir and ser share preterite forms: 'fui al banco' (I went) and 'fue difícil' (it was difficult)
Regularising irregular preterites: 'yo hací' should be 'yo hice'
Confusing -ar nosotros present and preterite: 'hablamos' can be present or preterite — context determines which
tip: Time markers help identify the preterite: ayer (yesterday), anoche (last night), la semana pasada (last week), el año pasado (last year), en 2023, hace dos días (two days ago). If you see these, use the preterite.
